# PEPH Artifact Documentation

This document describes **all artifacts produced by a PEPH pipeline run**, their structure, and how they should be interpreted.

A pipeline execution produces a **fully reproducible run directory** under:

models/

Each run is written to a timestamped folder:

models/<run_name>_<timestamp>/

Example:

models/seer_crc_peph_v1_2026-03-05_1124/

Artifacts are organized to support:

• reproducibility  
• post-hoc analysis  
• reporting  
• model inspection  
• debugging  
• downstream research workflows  

The pipeline is designed so that **everything needed to understand the model fit is stored in the run directory**.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1. Run Directory Layout

A typical run directory looks like:

Mode                 LastWriteTime         Length Name
----                 -------------         ------ ----
d-----                plots
d-----                predictions
d-----                tables

-a----                baseline_table.parquet
-a----                coef_table.parquet
-a----                config_resolved.yml
-a----                cox_snell_residuals.parquet
-a----                frailty_summary.json
-a----                frailty_table.parquet
-a----                inference.json
-a----                long_test.parquet
-a----                long_train.parquet
-a----                metrics.json
-a----                model.json
-a----                spatial_autocorr.json
-a----                split_ids.json
-a----                test_wide.parquet
-a----                train_wide.parquet

The artifacts can be grouped into:

1. model artifacts  
2. data artifacts  
3. evaluation artifacts  
4. spatial diagnostics  
5. plots  

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. Model Artifacts

These describe the **fitted statistical model**.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2.1 model.json

The primary serialized model artifact.

This file is sufficient to **reload the fitted model**.

Loadable with:

FittedPEPHModel.load(path)

Contents include:

• model type (PH or spatial)  
• regression coefficients  
• baseline hazards  
• feature encoding metadata  
• spatial frailty parameters (if present)  

Example structure:

{
  "model_type": "peph_leroux",
  "beta": {...},
  "baseline": {...},
  "encoding": {...},
  "spatial": {
      "area_col": "zip",
      "zips": [...],
      "u": [...],
      "rho": 0.62,
      "tau": 1.8
  }
}

This file is the **canonical model representation**.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2.2 coef_table.parquet

Table of regression coefficients.

Columns:

feature  
coef  
std_err  
z  
p_value  
ci_lower  
ci_upper  

These correspond to the regression terms in the PH model.

Categorical variables appear as dummy variables.

Example:

feature        coef     std_err     z     p_value
age            0.034    0.004
stage_III      0.52     0.09
sex_M         -0.11     0.05

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2.3 baseline_table.parquet

Estimated baseline hazards by interval.

Columns:

interval_start  
interval_end  
baseline_log_hazard  
baseline_hazard  

These define the piecewise constant baseline hazard.

Used for:

• prediction  
• cumulative hazard computation  

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2.4 inference.json

Summary of model inference configuration.

Includes:

• covariance estimator  
• optimization settings  
• convergence flags  

Example:

{
  "covariance": "cluster_id",
  "optimizer": "lbfgs",
  "converged": true
}

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. Data Artifacts

These describe the **training and testing data used in the model**.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3.1 train_wide.parquet

Training dataset in wide format.

One row per subject.

Contains:

id  
time  
event  
covariates  

Used for:

• debugging  
• re-fitting models  
• reproducing training data  

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3.2 test_wide.parquet

Test dataset used for prediction and evaluation.

Contains identical columns as training set.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3.3 long_train.parquet

Expanded long-format training data.

Columns include:

id  
interval  
exposure_time  
event  
covariates  

Used internally for the Poisson likelihood.

Saved for transparency and debugging.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3.4 long_test.parquet

Long-format expansion of the test set.

Used during prediction.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3.5 split_ids.json

Defines the train/test split.

Structure:

{
  "train_ids": [...],
  "test_ids": [...]
}

Ensures exact reproducibility of model evaluation.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 4. Evaluation Artifacts

These quantify model performance.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4.1 metrics.json

Summary of predictive performance.

Includes:

• time-dependent AUC  
• concordance index  
• Brier score  
• calibration intercept  
• calibration slope  

Example:

{
  "c_index": 0.71,
  "auc_365": 0.72,
  "auc_730": 0.70,
  "brier_365": 0.18,
  "calibration_intercept": -0.03,
  "calibration_slope": 0.98
}

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4.2 cox_snell_residuals.parquet

Cox–Snell residuals for model diagnostics.

Columns:

id  
residual  

Used for checking:

• goodness-of-fit  
• model calibration  

Residuals should approximately follow an exponential distribution.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 5. Spatial Frailty Artifacts

Produced only when the **spatial backend** is used.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5.1 frailty_table.parquet

Estimated spatial random effects.

Columns:

zip  
u_hat  
hazard_multiplier  
component  

Where:

hazard_multiplier = exp(u_hat)

Interprets the multiplicative effect of spatial frailty.

Example:

zip      u_hat     hazard_multiplier
30909    0.0059    1.006
30101   -0.0225    0.978

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5.2 frailty_summary.json

Summary statistics of the frailty distribution.

Includes:

• mean frailty  
• standard deviation  
• quantiles  

Example:

{
  "mean": 0.0,
  "sd": 0.07,
  "min": -0.23,
  "max": 0.19
}

---------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5.3 spatial_autocorr.json

Spatial autocorrelation diagnostics.

Includes:

Moran's I statistic

Example:

{
  "morans_I": 0.21,
  "p_value": 0.003
}

Positive values indicate spatial clustering.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 6. Predictions Directory

Directory:

predictions/

Contains prediction tables for the test set.

Typical files:

risk_predictions.parquet  
survival_predictions.parquet  

Each row corresponds to a subject.

Example columns:

id  
risk_365  
risk_730  
risk_1825  

Used for evaluation metrics.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 7. Tables Directory

Directory:

tables/

Contains exported summary tables for reporting.

Typical outputs:

coef_table.csv  
frailty_table.csv  

Generated by reporting utilities.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 8. Plots Directory

Directory:

plots/

Contains visualization outputs.

Examples:

calibration plots  
Cox–Snell diagnostic plots  
frailty caterpillar plots  
spatial maps (if implemented)

Plots are generated using matplotlib.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 9. Configuration Artifact

## 9.1 config_resolved.yml

Final resolved configuration used for the run.

Includes:

• default values  
• user overrides  

This ensures the run can be reproduced exactly.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 10. Reproducibility Guarantee

A PEPH run directory contains everything required to reproduce the analysis:

configuration  
training data  
test data  
model parameters  
evaluation metrics  

Given a run directory, one can:

• reload the model  
• regenerate predictions  
• inspect diagnostics  
• reproduce reported metrics  

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 11. Best Practices

When analyzing results:

Always keep run directories immutable.

Never overwrite artifacts.

Use reporting utilities to generate summaries rather than modifying artifacts.

For research projects, store run directories under version control or archival storage.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

# 12. Planned Future Artifacts

Upcoming features may introduce additional artifacts:

baseline_spline_table.parquet  
nonph_beta_table.parquet  
treatment_effect_summary.json  

These will follow the same artifact conventions described here.
